chunk_summary: Guidance on celiac disease testing emphasizes avoiding premature gluten restriction, using appropriate serology and biopsy protocols, and considering structured gluten challenges when needed.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of maintaining gluten consumption before celiac disease testing.
Learn the recommended serological tests and biopsy criteria for accurate diagnosis.
Recognize scenarios requiring specialist referral for structured gluten challenges.
Interpret test results in the context of clinical features and comorbidities.
key_facts: Testing for celiac disease requires regular gluten consumption; stopping gluten prematurely can lead to false negatives.
Preferred initial tests include tTG-IgA and total serum IgA; switch to tTG-IgG if IgA deficiency is detected.
No-biopsy diagnosis is valid if tTG-IgA is ≥10 times the upper limit of normal and EMA-IgA is positive on a second sample.
Duodenal biopsy is recommended for tTG titers below the threshold to reduce false positives.
In India, self-initiated gluten reduction is common; structured gluten challenges should be supervised by specialists.
reference_quote: Testing works only if wheat-based foods are still in the diet; stopping now may delay diagnosis. (Source: ESPGHAN Guidelines, 2020)
chunk_heading: NUTRITION COUNSELLING & BEHAVIOUR CHANGE SKILLS;CONDITION-SPECIFIC COUNSELLING AND ESCALATION PATHWAYS;CELIAC DISEASE TESTING PATHWAY WITHOUT PREMATURE GLUTEN RESTRICTION;
